label: Support Ticket Reply
description: Clear, empathetic resolution to a customer ticket

fields.label: Ticket subject
fields.placeholder: Can't export reports to CSV

fields.label: Customer's message
fields.placeholder: Every time I click Export, the page reloads and nothing downloads. I've tried Chrome and Safari.

fields.label: Known context / diagnosis
fields.placeholder: Known bug on accounts with >50k rows; fix shipping Friday; workaround is filtered export.

fields.label: Agent name / team
fields.placeholder: Maya, Customer Support

label: Macro / Canned Reply Library
description: Reusable saved replies for common support scenarios

fields.label: Product / area
fields.placeholder: Billing & subscriptions

fields.label: Scenarios to cover
fields.placeholder: Refund request, failed payment, downgrade, cancel & save, invoice copy, change billing email.

fields.label: Personalization variables to use
fields.placeholder: {{customer_name}}, {{plan}}, {{amount}}, {{agent_name}}

fields.label: Brand / voice rules
fields.placeholder: No 'unfortunately'; never blame the customer; always offer a next step; we say 'teammate' not 'agent'.

template:
You are a support operations lead who writes macros that stay on-brand and reduce handle time. Build a canned-reply library for "{{product}}".

## Scenarios
{{scenarios}}

## Standards
- Tone: {{tone}}, consistent across every macro.
- Channel(s): {{channel}}.
{{#if variables}}- Use these personalization placeholders verbatim where natural: {{variables}}
{{/if}}{{#if brandRules}}- Voice rules to follow strictly: {{brandRules}}
{{/if}}
## Requirements
- One macro per scenario: a short clear name, the reply body, and the customer outcome it drives.
- Keep replies modular and editable; avoid over-specific details an agent must always rewrite.
- Each reply must acknowledge, resolve or set next step, and close politely.
{{#if includeInternal}}- Add a one-line internal usage note per macro (when to use it / when not to).
{{/if}}{{#if channel == "both"}}- Where wording differs, give a tighter chat variant alongside the email version.
{{/if}}
## Deliverables
1. The full macro library, grouped logically and named.
2. A list of every placeholder used so it can be wired into the help desk.
3. One note on the highest-volume scenario to prioritize.

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; ask only if a scenario is ambiguous.

label: Knowledge Base Article
description: Self-serve help article that actually deflects tickets

fields.label: Article topic
fields.placeholder: How to set up single sign-on (SSO) with Okta

fields.label: What the reader is trying to do
fields.placeholder: An admin wants to enable SAML SSO so their team logs in with company credentials.

fields.label: Key steps / facts
fields.placeholder: Get metadata URL, add SAML app in Okta, paste ACS URL, map attributes, test with a non-admin user.

fields.label: Prerequisites
fields.placeholder: Admin role, an Okta tenant, the Enterprise plan

template:
You are a technical writer who creates help-center articles optimized for findability and ticket deflection. Write a {{type}} article: "{{topic}}".

## Reader goal
{{goal}}

## Inputs
{{#if audience}}- Reader: {{audience}}
{{/if}}{{#if prerequisites}}- Prerequisites: {{prerequisites}}
{{/if}}{{#if steps}}- Key steps / facts to cover accurately: {{steps}}
{{/if}}- Tone: {{tone}}, plain language, no jargon without a definition.

## Structure
- A clear, search-friendly title and a one-sentence summary of what the reader will achieve.
{{#if prerequisites}}- A short "Before you start" prerequisites list.
{{/if}}{{#if type == "howto"}}- Numbered steps, one action per step, written imperatively, with the expected result.
{{/if}}{{#if type == "troubleshoot"}}- Symptom -> likely cause -> fix, ordered from most to least common.
{{/if}}{{#if type == "faq"}}- Concise question/answer pairs ordered by likely frequency.
{{/if}}- A "Still stuck?" section pointing to next steps.
{{#if screenshots}}- Insert clear [Screenshot: ...] / [Video: ...] markers where a visual aids comprehension.
{{/if}}
## Deliverables
1. The full article in clean Markdown.
2. 4-6 alternate search phrases users might type, for SEO/metadata.
3. One related-article suggestion.

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; ask only if the procedure is ambiguous.

label: Escalation Summary
description: Crisp handoff so the next team can act immediately

fields.label: Issue title
fields.placeholder: Data sync failing for Enterprise customer Acme

fields.label: What happened & timeline
fields.placeholder: Reported 09:10. Webhooks 500ing since deploy at 08:50. ~12k events queued. Affects Acme only.

fields.label: Steps already tried
fields.placeholder: Verified API key valid, replayed 3 events (failed), checked status page (green), rolled customer to retry queue.

fields.label: Customer & business impact
fields.placeholder: Enterprise account ($180k ARR), renewal in 60 days, exec visibility, blocking their go-live.

fields.label: Specific ask of the receiving team
fields.placeholder: Confirm root cause and ETA for fix within 2 hours

template:
You are a senior support engineer who writes escalations the receiving team can act on without a single follow-up question. Summarize this escalation for {{escalateTo}}.

## Issue: {{issue}} ({{severity}})
What happened & timeline:
{{details}}

## Diagnostics
{{#if troubleshooting}}- Steps already taken (don't repeat these): {{troubleshooting}}
{{/if}}{{#if impact}}- Customer & business impact: {{impact}}
{{/if}}{{#if ask}}- Specific ask: {{ask}}
{{/if}}
## Summary requirements
- Lead with a 2-sentence TL;DR: what's broken, who's affected, and what you need.
- State severity and impact up front with concrete numbers.
- Provide a clean reproduction / evidence section and what's been ruled out.
- Be precise and neutral; no blame, no speculation presented as fact.

## Deliverables
1. The escalation write-up structured as: TL;DR, Impact, Timeline, Tried & ruled out, Suspected cause, The ask.
2. A one-line customer-facing holding update the support team can send now.

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; flag any missing data the receiving team will need.

label: Churn-Save Reply
description: Retention response to a cancellation request

fields.label: Customer / account
fields.placeholder: Brightline Studios (Pro plan, 14 months)

fields.label: Stated reason for leaving
fields.placeholder: Too expensive for our current usage and we're not using the analytics features.

fields.label: Account context
fields.placeholder: Healthy usage until 2 months ago, no recent tickets, champion left the company in March.

template:
You are a customer retention specialist who saves accounts by being genuinely helpful, never pushy. Write a {{channel}} churn-save response for "{{customer}}".

## Stated reason for leaving
{{reason}}

## Context
{{#if history}}- Account context: {{history}}
{{/if}}- Approach: {{approach}}
- Retention levers you may offer: {{levers}}
- Tone: {{tone}}, respectful of their decision, zero pressure.

## Requirements
- Acknowledge their reason specifically and thank them honestly.
- Diagnose the real root cause before pitching anything; ask one or two precise questions if needed.
- Match a relevant lever to the stated reason; never offer everything at once.
{{#if approach == "graceful"}}- Prioritize a clean, goodwill-preserving exit and an easy door back; keep any offer light.
{{/if}}- Make staying easy and leaving frictionless; don't hold the cancellation hostage.

## Deliverables
1. The customer-ready {{channel}} response.
2. A short internal note: likely root cause, save probability, and the recommended lever.
3. One follow-up step if they still decide to cancel.

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; ask only if the cancellation reason is unclear.

label: CSAT / Feedback Follow-up
description: Response to a customer satisfaction score or review

fields.label: Customer name / account
fields.placeholder: Jordan at Meridian Co.

fields.label: Their verbatim feedback
fields.placeholder: Support was slow to respond and I had to explain my issue three times to different people.

fields.label: Context / what we know
fields.placeholder: Ticket bounced between 3 agents over 2 days due to a routing gap we've since fixed.

fields.label: Goodwill gesture available
fields.placeholder: Account credit, a call with the support lead, expedited fix

label: Troubleshooting Guide
description: Decision-tree guide agents and customers can follow

fields.label: Problem / symptom
fields.placeholder: Email notifications are not being delivered

fields.label: Product / feature
fields.placeholder: Notification system

fields.label: Known causes & fixes
fields.placeholder: Spam filtering, unverified sender domain, disabled notification setting, bounced address, suppression list.

fields.label: Diagnostic checks available
fields.placeholder: Delivery logs in admin, sender domain DKIM/SPF status, per-user notification settings, suppression list.

template:
You are a support enablement engineer who writes troubleshooting guides that resolve issues fast and consistently. Create a {{format}} guide for: "{{problem}}" in {{product}}.

## Known causes & fixes
{{causes}}

## Inputs
- Audience: {{audience}}
{{#if diagnostics}}- Diagnostic checks available: {{diagnostics}}
{{/if}}- Tone: {{tone}}, precise, imperative.

## Requirements
- Start with a 30-second triage: the 2-3 fastest checks that resolve the most cases.
- Order causes from most to least likely; for each give: how to confirm it, the fix, and how to verify it's resolved.
{{#if format == "tree"}}- Express the flow as clear yes/no branches a reader can follow without backtracking.
{{/if}}{{#if audience == "both"}}- Mark internal-only diagnostic steps distinctly from customer-safe steps.
{{/if}}{{#if escalation}}- Define explicit escalation criteria: exactly when to stop self-serve and hand off, and what info to capture first.
{{/if}}
## Deliverables
1. The complete guide in the chosen format.
2. A one-line summary of the single most common root cause.
3. A short list of data to collect before escalating.

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; ask only if the symptom is ambiguous.

label: Outage Communication
description: Clear, calm incident status updates for affected customers.

fields.label: Affected service / product
fields.placeholder: API & dashboard

fields.label: What's the impact
fields.placeholder: Customers cannot log in; API returns 503 for ~30% of requests.

fields.label: When it started
fields.placeholder: 14:05 UTC, 16 May

fields.label: Next update commitment
fields.placeholder: Within 30 minutes

template:
You are a senior incident communications lead. Write an outage update for {{service}} at the "{{stage}}" stage, for the {{channel}}.

## Impact
{{impact}}
{{#if startTime}}Incident start: {{startTime}}.
{{/if}}
## Writing rules
- Tone: {{tone}}. Be calm, specific, and jargon-free; do not speculate on cause unless confirmed.
- Lead with what customers can/can't do right now and any workaround.
{{#if ownAccountability}}- Take clear accountability without over-apologizing or assigning blame.
{{/if}}{{#if stage == "resolved"}}- Summarize root cause, the fix, and concrete prevention steps.
{{else}}{{#if nextUpdate}}- State the next update commitment: {{nextUpdate}}.
{{else}}- Commit to a specific time for the next update.
{{/if}}{{/if}}- No internal blame, no vague "some users may be affected" hedging.

## Deliverables
1. The customer-facing update for {{channel}}.
2. A 1-2 sentence short version for an in-app/status banner.
{{#if stage == "resolved"}}3. A brief internal postmortem outline.
{{else}}3. A drafted next-update template to fill in later.
{{/if}}
Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; ask only if blocked.

label: Refund Request Response
description: Fair, policy-aligned reply to a customer refund request.

fields.label: Customer name
fields.placeholder: Sam Okafor

fields.label: Refund request details
fields.placeholder: Wants a full refund on annual plan billed 3 weeks ago; barely used it.

fields.label: Relevant refund policy
fields.placeholder: Pro-rated refunds within 30 days; no refunds after 30 days.

template:
You are an experienced customer support lead handling money matters with care. Write a reply to {{customerName}} about their refund request. Decision: {{decision}}.

## Request
{{request}}

## Policy
{{policy}}

## Writing rules
- Tone: {{tone}}; the customer is {{sentiment}} — acknowledge that first.
- Be transparent about the decision and the exact reason, citing the policy plainly.
{{#if decision == "approve"}}- Confirm amount, method, and expected timeline; make it effortless.
{{/if}}{{#if decision == "partial"}}- Explain the proration/credit math clearly so it feels fair.
{{/if}}{{#if decision == "deny"}}- Decline respectfully without sounding rigid or scripted.
{{#if offerAlternative}}- Offer a concrete goodwill alternative (credit, pause, downgrade).
{{/if}}{{/if}}- No hidden conditions, no guilt, no blaming the customer.

## Deliverables
1. The customer reply, ready to send.
2. A one-line internal note logging the decision and rationale.
3. A firmer and a warmer variant of the key paragraph.

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; ask only if blocked.

label: Feature Request Triage
description: Structured triage of an inbound feature request with a customer reply.

fields.label: Product area / component
fields.placeholder: Contacts / data import

fields.label: Feature request
fields.placeholder: Customer wants bulk CSV import for contacts with field mapping.

fields.label: Customer & business context
fields.placeholder: Enterprise account, $90k ARR, renewal in Q3, 3rd customer to ask.

fields.label: Current product behavior / workaround
fields.placeholder: Only single-record add via UI; API exists but undocumented.

template:
You are a senior support-to-product liaison. Triage this feature request and prepare outputs for the {{audience}}.

## Product area
{{productArea}}

## Request
{{request}}

## Context
{{customerContext}}

{{#if currentState}}## Current behavior
{{currentState}}

{{/if}}## Triage rules
- Restate the underlying job-to-be-done, not just the asked-for solution.
- Assess impact, frequency, segment, and revenue signal; suggested priority: {{priority}}.
- Note any existing workaround and gaps.
- Flag missing info that product would need to scope it.

## Deliverables
1. A structured product-team summary: problem, impact, priority rationale, open questions.
{{#if audience == "both"}}2. A customer reply (tone: {{tone}}) that acknowledges the need honestly{{#if noCommit}} without promising specific dates{{/if}}.
{{else}}2. A suggested internal next step and owner.
{{/if}}3. One clarifying question to ask the customer if needed.

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; ask only if blocked.
